splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA's statutory measurement date requires all actuarial assumptions to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year, or whether actuaries retain discretion to select assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in withdrawal liability obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be determined as of the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary retains discretion to select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects how federal courts calculate sentencing at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled during the period a supervisee absconds, so the term does not run while the person is a fugitive from supervision.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to create such a doctrine.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) — which prevents relitigating previously raised claims — in addition to § 2255(h)'s requirements when bringing successive collateral attacks. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are categorically barred from re-raising any previously presented claim.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar on previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, imposing a stricter gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits have disagreed on the standard for when candidates have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. Some circuits grant candidates broad standing based on competitive injury, while others require a more concrete showing of harm. The resolution affects the ability to challenge mail-in voting rules and ballot-counting deadlines nationwide.

positions.label: Broad candidate standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge election rules that allegedly dilute lawful votes or change voting procedures, based on competitive injury.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Narrow candidate standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates must demonstrate a more particularized and concrete injury to establish Article III standing for pre-election challenges to voting procedures.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Jacobson v. Florida Secretary of State

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts should defer to the Sentencing Commission's or an agency's interpretation of an ambiguous criminal provision, or instead apply the rule of lenity in favor of the defendant. At least three circuits hold lenity trumps deference, while the Third Circuit has held that agency deference prevails. The issue has grown more significant after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer/Kisor or Chevron deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal or sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated interpretive power.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference prevails over the rule of lenity, allowing deference to anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision means all three listed criminal-history criteria must be met to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, or whether meeting any single criterion is disqualifying. The split results in defendants with identical criminal histories receiving significantly different sentences depending on where they are prosecuted.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or.'

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) includes inchoate offenses such as conspiracy, where the guideline text is silent but Application Note 1 to the commentary includes them.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor, or whether the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses. The split affects career-offender designations for defendants with prior conspiracy convictions.

positions.label: Commentary is authoritative — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of conspiracy in the definition of 'controlled substance offense' is entitled to deference as the Commission's authoritative interpretation of an ambiguous guideline.

positions.label: Guideline text is unambiguous — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b)'s text unambiguously does not include inchoate offenses, so Application Note 1 exceeds the Commission's interpretive authority and is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as a factor supporting compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others allow courts to consider them. The 2024 Sentencing Commission policy statement amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to resolve this, but circuits disagree on whether the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons under § 3582(c)(1)(A), and that the Sentencing Commission's policy statement attempting to allow this exceeds its statutory authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, including the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment, as one factor among others in evaluating extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state statute criminalizes a substance not listed on the federal Controlled Substances Act schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally controlled, or whether any state-controlled substance suffices. Some circuits hold there is no federal-match requirement, while others require the substance to be federally scheduled.

positions.label: No federal-match requirement
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include any offense under state law, even if the particular substance is not controlled under federal law.

positions.label: Federal-match requirement applies
positions.summary: Holds that a state conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the underlying substance is also controlled under federal law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and its conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, while other courts have reached the opposite conclusion, creating a split on the interpretation of § 348(f)(1)(A).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity belong to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, based on the broad scope of § 541(a)'s definition of property of the estate.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the debtor, not the estate, based on the structure and legislative history of § 348(f)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal habeas corpus under 28 U.S.C. § 2241 is available to challenge conditions of confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement), or whether such claims must be brought as civil rights actions.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas is the exclusive remedy only for challenges to the fact or duration of confinement, or whether it also extends to challenges to conditions of confinement. The Supreme Court noted but did not resolve this question in Preiser v. Rodriguez. The split has practical consequences for federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions, including First Step Act time-credit determinations.

positions.label: Habeas available for conditions challenges
positions.summary: Holds that habeas corpus under § 2241 is available to challenge not only the fact or duration of confinement but also certain conditions of confinement, particularly where the prisoner seeks release or transfer.

positions.label: Habeas limited to fact/duration of confinement
positions.summary: Holds that habeas corpus is available only to challenge the fact or duration of confinement, and that conditions-of-confinement claims must be pursued through civil rights statutes like Bivens or § 1983.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish a defendant for violations of supervised release at revocation, or whether revocation sanctions must be limited to addressing the defendant's breach of trust and the need for further supervision.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible purposes of a sentence imposed upon revocation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for supervised-release violations, while other circuits hold that revocation sentences should serve supervisory rather than punitive purposes. The split affects hundreds of revocation proceedings annually.

positions.label: Punishment is permissible at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release, treating revocation as akin to sentencing for new misconduct.

positions.label: Punishment is not the purpose of revocation
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sanctions should address the breach of the court's trust and the need for further supervision, not serve as punishment for the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an appeal from the denial of a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits have split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees, filed in a criminal case, is governed by the 30-day civil appeal deadline or the 14-day criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the Hyde Amendment motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) governs.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Manchester Farming Partnership

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises in a criminal case and is thus governed by the shorter criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents referencing private medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical information are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order.' Other circuits have not uniformly recognized such orders as collateral orders, creating potential divergence on when sealing denials qualify for immediate appellate review.

positions.label: Immediately appealable as collateral order
positions.summary: Holds that denials of motions to seal documents referencing medical treatments and diagnoses involve a value of 'high order' that warrants immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal do not meet the stringent requirements of the collateral order doctrine and must await final judgment for appellate review.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: In re City of New York

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to address the Second Circuit's approach to discrimination claims in the hospitality industry, indicating an unsettled or split legal framework regarding the standards for establishing discrimination liability and available remedies when patrons allege unfair treatment by business operators.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: Applied certain standards for evaluating discrimination claims against hospitality establishments that the Supreme Court found sufficiently important or unsettled to warrant review.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different or conflicting standards for evaluating discrimination claims under § 1981 in the context of service and hospitality establishments.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption provision, which bars state laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any ... broker,' preempts state-law negligence claims brought against freight brokers when the motor carrier they selected causes injury. The split has significant implications for the trucking and logistics industries.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because broker selection of carriers relates to the broker's 'services' within the meaning of the statute.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted by the FAAAA because such traditional tort claims do not relate to brokers' prices, routes, or services in the relevant sense.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus relief — such as alleged legal errors in the underlying conviction — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under the compassionate release statute. Some circuits permit consideration while others categorically bar it.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts have discretion to consider a broad range of factors, including potential legal errors in the conviction or sentence, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for challenging the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be raised through habeas corpus under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in New York State Rifle & Pistol Ass'n v. Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances, particularly marijuana, is consistent with the Second Amendment's text and the nation's historical tradition of firearms regulation.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that the historical tradition of disarming those who pose a danger to themselves or others, including habitual substance users, supports the constitutionality of § 922(g)(3) even after Bruen.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government cannot demonstrate a sufficient historical analogue for disarming non-violent drug users.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the scope of the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'engaged in . . . interstate commerce.' This affects the enforceability of arbitration agreements for a wide range of distribution workers.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are part of a continuous interstate transportation chain, including those making last-mile deliveries of goods that have moved in interstate commerce, qualify as transportation workers exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that distribution workers delivering goods locally to retail stores are not 'transportation workers engaged in . . . interstate commerce' under Section 1 because they are not themselves moving goods across state lines.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state-law failure-to-warn claims against pesticide manufacturers when the claims are based on labeling that the EPA approved. This affects product liability litigation involving agricultural chemicals and consumer pesticides nationwide.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling different from or in addition to what the EPA has approved.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims because they impose requirements 'parallel to' rather than 'in addition to' FIFRA's labeling requirements, consistent with the Supreme Court's Bates v. Dow Agrosciences framework.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
